transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Karen. It's nice to meet you. I’m here to listen and support you. What feels most important for you to talk about today?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi, um, thanks for having me. I guess I’ve been thinking a lot about my experiences at work lately. There’s been a lot of talk about culture and atmosphere, and I wanted to be part of those discussions, but... it feels like I keep missing out. I don’t know if my thoughts really matter in those conversations anyway. It’s just... I don’t know, disappointing, I guess.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Thank you for sharing that, Karen. It sounds like you're feeling disappointed about not being able to participate in those discussions at work, and there's a sense that your thoughts might not hold value in those conversations. That must be frustrating for you. Can you tell me more about what specifically makes you feel like you're missing out?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it is frustrating. I mean, I really want to contribute, but when I think about speaking up, I just feel like my ideas aren’t as important as others. It’s like, I want to join in, but then I think, “What’s the point?” I end up just going along with what others want, like meeting with clients instead. It’s hard to shake that feeling, you know? I just... I don’t want to feel lonely in those moments.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like there's a real struggle between your desire to contribute and the belief that your ideas might not be as important as others'. That feeling of loneliness when you hold back must be quite heavy. When you think about speaking up, what kind of thoughts or beliefs come up for you that make you feel like your ideas don’t matter?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I guess I just have this automatic thought that, you know, it doesn’t really matter if I share my perspective. I think about how others might have more experience or stronger opinions, and I just... I don’t want to take up space if it’s not worth it. It’s like I’m stuck in this loop where I feel sad about not participating, but then I convince myself that it’s better to stay quiet. It’s just confusing, I guess.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That does sound confusing, Karen. It seems like you're caught in a cycle where your desire to participate clashes with the belief that your voice isn’t as valuable as others. It must be tough to feel sad about missing out while also feeling like staying quiet is the safer option. When you think about those moments of wanting to share your perspective, what emotions come up for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, when I think about wanting to share, I feel a mix of sadness and disappointment. There’s this loneliness that creeps in, like I’m on the outside looking in. I also feel a bit anxious, like what if I say something and it’s not well-received? I just end up feeling down about it all. It’s hard to shake those feelings, especially when I really want to be part of the conversation. I guess I just wish I could feel more confident about my thoughts.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like those moments bring up a lot of heavy emotions for you—sadness, disappointment, loneliness, and anxiety. Wanting to be part of the conversation while feeling unsure about how your thoughts will be received can be really challenging. It’s understandable to wish for more confidence in those situations. When you think about feeling more confident, what do you imagine that would look or feel like for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hmm, I think if I felt more confident, I’d be able to share my ideas without second-guessing myself all the time. I’d feel more at ease in those discussions, maybe even excited to contribute. It would be nice to feel like my thoughts are valid and that they could add something meaningful to the conversation. I imagine I’d feel more connected to my colleagues, like I’m part of the team instead of just an observer. But right now, it feels like a long way off.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That vision of feeling confident and connected is really powerful, Karen. It sounds like being able to share your ideas without second-guessing would not only enhance your experience in those discussions but also strengthen your sense of belonging with your colleagues. It’s understandable to feel that it’s a long way off, especially when you’re currently facing those doubts. What do you think might help you take even small steps toward that feeling of confidence?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess, um, maybe starting with smaller conversations could help? Like, if I could practice sharing my thoughts in less formal settings or with people I feel a bit more comfortable with, that might ease some of the anxiety. I could also try reminding myself that my perspective is valid, even if it’s different from others. It’s just hard to break that cycle of doubt. I think if I could see that my ideas are valued, it might help me feel more confident over time. But it’s definitely a bit daunting to think about.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those sound like thoughtful and practical steps, Karen. Starting with smaller conversations and practicing in a more comfortable setting could be a great way to build your confidence gradually. It’s also important to remind yourself that your perspective is valid, even if it differs from others. It makes sense that seeing your ideas valued would help break that cycle of doubt. It’s completely normal to feel daunted by the process, especially when change takes time. How do you feel about taking those initial steps? What emotions come up for you when you think about trying this approach?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thinking about taking those initial steps makes me feel a bit nervous, to be honest. There’s a part of me that feels hopeful, like maybe I could actually do it, but then the anxiety kicks in. I worry about how I’ll be received or if I’ll stumble over my words. It’s a mix of excitement and fear, I guess. I want to push myself to try, but I also feel that familiar heaviness of doubt. It’s like I’m standing at the edge, wanting to jump in but feeling scared of the water.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That’s a really vivid way to describe it, Karen—standing at the edge, feeling both excitement and fear about jumping in. It’s completely natural to feel nervous when considering those initial steps, especially when you’re hoping for a positive experience but also grappling with doubt. It sounds like there’s a part of you that is ready to push forward, while another part is holding back out of fear. How do you think you might navigate that mix of emotions as you consider taking those steps?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think, um, acknowledging those mixed emotions might be a good start. Like, recognizing that it’s okay to feel nervous and that it doesn’t mean I can’t try. Maybe I could set small, achievable goals for myself, so it doesn’t feel so overwhelming. I could also remind myself that it’s a process, and it’s okay if it doesn’t go perfectly. Having some support from colleagues or even talking about it here might help me feel less alone in it. I just need to find a way to balance that fear with the hope of what could happen if I do take those steps.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a very thoughtful approach, Karen. Acknowledging those mixed emotions and recognizing that it’s okay to feel nervous is a great first step. Setting small, achievable goals could help make the process feel more manageable, and it’s wise to remind yourself that it’s a journey, not a race. Seeking support from colleagues and sharing your feelings here can also create a sense of connection and lessen that feeling of being alone in it. Balancing fear with hope is definitely a delicate dance. What do you think might be the first small goal you’d like to set for yourself as you begin this process?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe my first small goal could be to share one idea in a casual team meeting or even just with a colleague I trust. It doesn’t have to be anything big—just something I feel comfortable with. I could also try to prepare a little beforehand, so I feel more ready. If I can do that, it might help me build some confidence for the next time. I just want to take that first step without feeling too overwhelmed.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a very achievable first goal, Karen. Sharing one idea in a casual setting with a trusted colleague is a great way to ease into it. Preparing a bit beforehand can also help you feel more ready and confident. It’s wonderful that you’re thinking about taking that first step while being mindful of not overwhelming yourself. How do you feel about setting that goal? What emotions come up for you as you think about taking that action?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a mix of relief and nervousness about setting that goal. On one hand, it feels good to have something specific to aim for, and I’m hopeful that it could help me break the cycle of doubt. But on the other hand, I still feel that familiar anxiety creeping in, like what if I stumble or my idea isn’t well-received? It’s a bit scary to think about putting myself out there, but I also know that it’s a step towards feeling more connected and confident. I guess I just need to remind myself that it’s okay to feel both excited and scared at the same time.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely understandable to feel both relief and nervousness, Karen. Having a specific goal can bring a sense of direction, while the anxiety about potential outcomes is a natural response when stepping outside your comfort zone. Acknowledging that it’s okay to feel excited and scared at the same time shows a lot of self-awareness. It sounds like you’re really working to balance those emotions as you move forward. As you prepare to take this step, what might be a helpful reminder or mantra for yourself to keep that balance in mind?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think a helpful reminder for me might be something like, “It’s okay to be nervous; my voice matters.” That way, I can acknowledge the anxiety but also reinforce the idea that my thoughts and contributions are valid. I could also remind myself that it’s a learning experience, and it’s okay if it doesn’t go perfectly. Just taking that step is progress, and every little bit counts. I think having that mantra could help ground me when those nerves start to creep in.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That’s a beautiful mantra, Karen—“It’s okay to be nervous; my voice matters.” It captures both the acknowledgment of your feelings and the affirmation of your worth. Reminding yourself that it’s a learning experience and that progress comes in small steps is also a healthy perspective. It sounds like you’re setting yourself up for success by creating a supportive framework for yourself. How do you feel about having that mantra ready as you approach your goal? What impact do you think it might have on your experience?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Having that mantra ready feels comforting, actually. I think it could help me stay focused on what really matters—my voice and my contributions—rather than getting lost in the anxiety. It’s like a little anchor I can hold onto when I start to feel overwhelmed. I hope it’ll remind me that it’s okay to take risks and that even if things don’t go perfectly, I’m still making progress. Just having that positive reinforcement might help me feel a bit more confident as I approach my goal.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s wonderful to hear that the mantra feels comforting and like an anchor for you, Karen. Having that positive reinforcement can really help shift your focus back to what’s important—your voice and contributions. It’s great that you’re recognizing the value of taking risks and that progress is still progress, no matter how it looks. As you prepare to take this step, what do you think you might do to celebrate or acknowledge your efforts, regardless of the outcome?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it would be nice to celebrate even the small steps, like maybe treating myself to something I enjoy afterward, like a favorite snack or a little time to relax. I could also take a moment to reflect on what I did, regardless of how it went, and acknowledge that I took a risk and put myself out there. It’s important to recognize that effort, even if it doesn’t go exactly as planned. I think celebrating those moments could help reinforce the idea that I’m making progress and that my voice does matter.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a lovely plan, Karen. Treating yourself to something enjoyable and taking time to reflect on your experience can be a great way to acknowledge your efforts and reinforce the importance of your voice. Celebrating those small steps is a wonderful way to build confidence and recognize your progress, no matter the outcome. It’s clear that you’re putting a lot of thought into this process, and that’s really commendable. As you move forward, how do you feel about the journey ahead? What are you most looking forward to as you take these steps?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a mix of hope and apprehension about the journey ahead. I’m looking forward to the possibility of feeling more connected to my colleagues and maybe even discovering that my ideas can contribute to the discussions. It’s exciting to think that I might start to break free from that feeling of being trapped and underappreciated. At the same time, I know there will be challenges, and I might still feel nervous. But I guess I’m hopeful that each step will help me build that confidence and remind me that my voice does matter. I’m ready to see where this takes me.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That’s a beautiful perspective, Karen. It’s wonderful to hear that you’re feeling hopeful about the potential for connection and contribution, while also acknowledging the challenges that may arise. Embracing both the excitement and the apprehension shows a lot of courage and self-awareness. It sounds like you’re ready to embark on this journey of growth, and each step you take will help reinforce the value of your voice. As you move forward, remember that it’s okay to feel a range of emotions along the way. I’m here to support you as you explore this path. Is there anything else on your mind that you’d like to discuss today?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you, I really appreciate your support. I think I’ve shared a lot about my thoughts and feelings today, and it feels good to have that space. I guess I just want to make sure I keep this momentum going and not let those doubts creep back in too much. Maybe we can talk more about strategies to keep that confidence up in our next session? For now, I think I’m okay. Just feeling a bit lighter after talking about it all.
